speaker: interviewer
text: December 21, 1995. The survivor's name is Ira Kaminsky. My name is Daniel Sedlis. We're in Bayside, New York, in the United States. And the language of the interview is English. Today's date is December 21, 1995. I'm interviewing Mr. Ira Kaminsky. My name is Daniel Sedlis. And we're in Bayside, New York, in the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you been known by any other names during your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name was, in Poland, Israel-- Israel in Hebrew. Kaminsky the same-- Kaminsky name I had, only I think it was-- the I was on the end instead of Y.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Chmielnik, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chmielnik. C-H. You have to pronounce 'chhh,' which we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that the Polish spelling that you gave?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And near what large city is Chmielnik?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were between Warsaw and Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born April 15, 1919, on Passover, on the first day of Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you brought up in Chmielnik?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was going to Polish school. But first, when I was a kid, at three, I started the cheder. And, of course, I had to learn Hebrew, how to read, and the prayers. My mother was very particular in these-- in these things, that a son should know Hebrew, and learn the Talmud, and learn that is the Bible, and the holiest things. And that's what I did. Then, at that age of 10, nine and a half, I started Polish school also. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I graduated Polish school, which I was-- I liked it. I liked the studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you went to cheder, cheder when you were three and a half. What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Yiddish. And who was in your home? Who was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- my father, my mother-- and we were 10 children in our family, a large family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to name the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my oldest-- was it-- my oldest sister, Hannah--

speaker: interviewee
text: --she was the oldest. Then the second one is Herschl. Here, it-- which is-- he is here in the United States. His name is, here, Harry Kaminsky. Then it was Frida. Then, after Frida, I was the fourth one. After me was Hinda. After Hinda was Rachel. She survived. She's now in Israel. Then Hene, a child. Then Mendel. The last were twins, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Tobe and Simcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke Yiddish to all--

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke Yiddish. Later on, I learned Hebrew, spoke in Hebrew. The [NON-ENGLISH], that was spoken in Hebrew. But all is-- home, we spoke only in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father was in the communication. He was-- he had a truck and owns a taxi. He supported the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and you said he had a taxi. This was a automotive vehicle--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are many automotive vehicles in Chmielnik already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, not too many. Private automotive vehicles, nobody had any private.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my uncle was, you know, very well-off. And he had some automobiles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father was working, also, for him, at times. And that's how he supported the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- your family was relatively well-off, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sometimes, when my father was out there-- working man, was a businessman. Sometimes business were all right, were good. But once we had an accident, and the truck broke down. And we were left without any, any income. And we had pretty rough times, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, we were not as bad as some, some, some people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a home did you live in? How, you know, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our, our home, we-- my father and my mother were traditional Orthodox. You know, we had, you know-- when we were small, we had to say the prayers in the morning and evening prayers. And especially my mother. My father was always busy, you know, to bring in, to support the family. My mother, she was very particular in these things, that the children should know these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the house like, physically? What was the nature of the structure?

speaker: interviewer
text: The physical structure of the house. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we were lucky. You know, we had our own house.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had all the two tenants, you know? And we, we were-- we lived, you know, in-- pretty, pretty nice in those days, considering those days, comparing to the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the Jewish community of Chmielnik? How large was it? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community was about-- before the war, was about over 8,000, and gentiles, 2,000, about 10,000 altogether. And it was really a Jewish, all Jewish businesses, you know, and-- mostly. And we, we had a very fine Jewish life that we didn't feel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we felt like if we were home, you know? We, we had, specially after World War I, the [NON-ENGLISH], instead going to yeshivas, they mostly were active people. And they, they formed. And they joined Zionist or Socialist movements. And they found their social life around these organizations, including me. I was, even while I was in school, I joined all the Zionist movement. And I was very active [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the Zionist movements that you belonged to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were several. You had all of them in Zionist movement. You had Socialist Zionists. And you had a general Zionist, a religious Zionist. And I belonged to the Betar.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the nationalist movement, which they are-- they are-- they were interested to train the Jewish young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They should be ready for Aliyah, to go to Israel and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even fight for the creation of a Jewish state.

speaker: interviewee
text: The majority of the young people in our city were the same opinion as I was, except socialists. Also, it didn't bother me, as long as they were good Jewish people. And they were interested mostly of Aliyah. They didn't see any future, those days, to stay in Poland, especially before-- between the two wars and in the 1930s, where anti-Semitism went rampant. And that's why the Jewish young people didn't see any future staying in Poland. The problem was that we couldn't go. We tried illegal Aliyah. But the English Mandate turned them down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in your own experience, what were the different political ideologies that you came in contact with in Chmielnik, within Zionism. Were there-- were there different factions that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had a different faction, like I said before. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every one had its own organization, its own youth, and its own surrounding. And between the political organizations, there was some fights, also, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but we forgot about it. This was part of our life, you know? Everybody wanted to show and to convince the people their own way. Well, what was your ideology?

speaker: interviewee
text: --own ideology was-- you see, when I went to school, I had a very fine teacher that-- I liked him. And he liked, me, too. We had always political discussions. And he was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was still in Chmielnik?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chmielnik, yes. And he was also officer of the Polish Army. And he convinced us-- don't think that you can have a Jewish state that you are-- that you want to create without fighting--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for it. And this made a tremendous impression on me. And I joined the organization that said that only through fighting-- not just buying a little, a little part of then Palestine, you will not have a Jewish state. And we succeeded very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I became an instructor. And they sent me to a special school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was in 1937. At the age of 18, they saw that I am material for a future instructor. And I went to a very fine school over there. We had the finest professors from England, from London. We had from Jerusalem, university professors. We had, from Lodz, a history professor, very fine. Professor [NON-ENGLISH] was very, very-- one of the best. And this was-- they opened my eyes. They opened a new world for me of knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the school located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was in Lodz. This was the second city-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second great city in Poland. In Lodz, there were about 200,000 Jewish people. And we had all at that school. I was trained as a military instructor. And I didn't mind. I liked it. And then, after I was finished--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1938, you said, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1938, '39, I came home. And I was teaching the young people over there discipline, military--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chmielnik. And then, cultural, also-- I had a group. Everyone-- every instructor, had a group, 10, 20, 30 people, and were teaching them various things-- literature and, you know, how to be a fine, good Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me some examples of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- everything. I, I like-- I love Jewish literature, starting from Sholem Aleichem and Sholem Asch, [NON-ENGLISH], and all the others, you know, and poets-- various. And we taught the people over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not just Jewish literature. I was also very much interested-- Polish, but mostly, then, I was in the Russian literature.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and all-- and, you know, and we, in, in the party, we had to learn. And we had to read, most-- we had to read. I, I, I read-- those days, I read all of Shakespeare, even. It was English.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did you read Shakespeare?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I read mostly in Polish or Yiddish, and little in Hebrew, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you read the Russian literature in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian literature was in Yiddish or in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very interesting. Then I had-- I had a friend of mine, came from Kielce. And he was a very intelligent man.

speaker: interviewee
text: He opened, for me, a new-- a new field of the Western writers-- English, French. Victor Hugo was my favorite [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about the French Revolution, and all the others. And it was-- it was really-- it was really a very interesting life. Social life was developed very, very nice-- very interesting during those days, until 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Before 1939 came, let me know a little bit about your family, in that were you the only one who had these kind of intellectual leanings, or, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's a matter of fact, I came-- I was already, willing or unwilling-- my sister, the older sister, Hannah--

speaker: interviewee
text: --she was the one who started all these things. She was involved-- and my brother Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were involved in playing theater--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish theater. And then-- and they-- and not just from, from Jewish books, Jewish, Jewish pieces, as they call it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they called it, but from French, from English, from a various-- languages. And they played theater. And as-- me, as a-- as a little boy-- and I was in love with that, even. And these repetitions I used to attend very, very often. And, it's a matter of fact, later on, I played the theater, too, with Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this kind of-- your sister and brother kindled--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always books in our house. There was always discussions--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my brother was a very good football player.

speaker: interviewee
text: Football was soccer. That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those days we called it football-- a soccer player. And I had a-- I used to-- my brother used to play in various cities. And I used to go with him as a young boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And I-- no, I was with him to help him. I had to see. You know, I was involved, also, in his plays, you know, in sports.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents share your ideologies?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents didn't. No, they didn't belong.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't-- I would say they didn't discourage me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not discourage. They saw it-- all their children are going, and growing up, and going the right way, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they became, you know, very intelligent, and all these things. I would like to say one more thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I graduated Polish school. And I had younger brothers and sisters from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very busy man.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my job to take care at the other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six other children that I was responsible-- and I encourage because I knew all the teachers from before, when I was graduated. And they all went to school. And they were good students, the children. It was-- even, it came to, sometimes, to a problem, that if somebody did something wrong, they didn't say, wait till the daddy will come home. They said, wait till Israel will come home. Then you'll see-- if they-- if they did something wrong. And I thank God they were raised, and they went to school, and they were good students, all six younger kids from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the Jews and the Gentiles in your community before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we was-- our social life was separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polish had a separate social life. We had, also, contacts, of course, with them. Wherever we used to live, we had Polish neighbors. And we were friendly with them. But socialize, we did only with our own Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your father's customers Polish? The clients of the transportation business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- yes, we had also Polish. Because the chauffeur was Polish, you know? The partner he had once-- he had once a partner, Polish partner. And we were very friendly, no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there anti-Semitic incidents in the school at times, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a problem while I was in school. There was a pogrom in Przytyk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish people, at that time, all over Poland, were very, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aggravated about that. And I had to do some work to complete some, some assignment. And I wrote about this pogrom in Przytyk. And I expressed my opinion, the Jewish opinion, in the way that some Polish people are behaving towards, towards the Jewish people. They don't-- they don't talk. They just fight. And the pogrom was-- made a a terrible impression on all of us that time. I was not afraid of the school, although we had teachers, anti-Semites, that I had, also, in my class once. He expressed some anti-Semitic views.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very-- it was insulting.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are a-- he used a certain expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say it in Polish, or Yiddish. I mean, he spoke it in Polish to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are so-- in-- so, so much Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in, in, in a-- in, in, in a way that he meant to insult.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the bones, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the marrow of, of, of, of the bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the marrow of the bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was president of my class. And that's all I had to do, that I gave a sign. He was from-- he was teaching us singing, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody-- the majority were Jewish people in our class. 80%, you know, were Jewish people in our city. And nobody was singing. And he noticed that. And he didn't know what, what happened. But he noticed that they were looking at me, at my sign. And he called  me out. He hit me and threw me out school. And he told me I should go. And I'll call-- I should call my father. He wants to talk to my father. Well, the whole city knew all about it, about the insult. And I, I, I, I, I asked my father even not to go-- and no apology. He shouldn't-- any apology. The only one who has to apologize was him. But he never apologized. But he learned a lesson, not to insult the Jewish students.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1933, when Hitler came to power, were you-- did this have an impact on you? The rise of Nazism in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rise of Nazism in Germany was very-- the Jewish people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knew that they were dangerous. They expected danger. They were not too familiar with Nazism. They knew the Germans from World War I. But some of them ignored them. We, the young people, of course, we couldn't take it. We were aware that we were fighting Nazism and ideas from Nazism. The problem was it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Against Nazism, against anti-Semitism. Because those days, the Polish people-- one wing, the nationalists, [POLISH], they adopted many things from Nazis, from the German Nazis. And the anti-Semitism became unbearable. Even that time, the Prime Minister, Skladkowski, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he didn't allow pogroms. He didn't allow beating of Jews. But fighting economically, he said, [SPEAKING POLISH], this you can do. And, well, that time in our city, in all, all the-- all the cities, you know, anti-Semitism became rampant. Don't buy from a Jew. Don't support the Jewish business system. Don't-- just buy from Polish merchants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did this impact your father's business? Did you feel this personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this-- it, it did not affect. But, you see, how did it affect my father, you're asking me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once-- he had a taxi that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- it wasn't like here, that, that every taxi had to, to go through a, a, a once a year. Over there, they came, all of a sudden, to the marketplace, where my father was, and the other taxis. They checked all the taxis that there were over there. They left all go, but my father's taxi--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the government, special agents. And they took off the license plates. And we couldn't-- we couldn't do anything. And after a while, we had to go through. It wasn't easy, you know, to, to register again. A Jew-- that's what a Jew went through over there, especially. He didn't  bother the Polish people. They didn't bother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any thoughts, at the time, in your family, or any realistic plan to leave Poland. I know you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we-- I wanted to leave very badly, very badly. It's a matter of fact, I don't know if I mentioned, no, my sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we used to call her Hannah-- 'Hancha.' She, she, she became a pioneer, Halutza.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Halutza, they went to a kibbutz that time, to prepare themselves--

speaker: interviewee
text: A kibbutz in Poland, to work hard at various trades, and various things, the hardest things, and support themselves. And then, after a year, they could go to, then, Palestine. And my sister was-- she was-- that was in 1933. She came back from a kibbutz. And she went for Aliyah to then Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.
